theorems.title: Decision lists generalize k-CNF and k-DNF
theorems.section: 3.1 Decision lists generalize k-CNF and k-DNF formulae
theorems.statementHtml: For \(0 < k < n\), \(k\text{-CNF}(n)\) and \(k\text{-DNF}(n)\) are proper subsets of \(k\text{-DL}(n)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
To see that \(k\)-DNF is a subset of \(k\)-DL, observe that we can easily transform a given formula in \(k\)-DNF into a decision list representing the same function. Each term of a DNF formula is converted into an item of a decision list with value 1. For example, the term \(x_1\overline{x}_2\) would yield the item \((x_1\overline{x}_2,1)\). The last (default) item of the decision list has the value 0.

To see that \(k\)-CNF is a subset of \(k\)-DL, recall that every \(k\)-CNF formula is the complement of some \(k\)-DNF formula, and that \(k\)-DL is closed under complementation.

The subset relations are proper, since neither \(k\)-CNF nor \(k\)-DNF is a subset of the other if \(0 < k < n\). ■

theorems.title: Decision lists generalize bounded-size CNF and DNF
theorems.section: 3.1 Decision lists generalize k-CNF and k-DNF formulae
theorems.statementHtml: For \(0 < k < n\), \(k\text{-clause-CNF}(n)\) and \(k\text{-term-DNF}(n)\) are proper subsets of \(k\text{-DL}(n)\).
theorems.proofHtml: This follows from the previous theorem and the previously noted facts that \(k\text{-clause-CNF}(n)\) is a subset of \(k\text{-DNF}(n)\), and that \(k\text{-term-DNF}(n)\) is a subset of \(k\text{-CNF}(n)\). ■

theorems.title: Decision lists properly generalize the union of k-CNF and k-DNF
theorems.section: 3.1 Decision lists generalize k-CNF and k-DNF formulae
theorems.statementHtml: For \(0 < k < n\) and \(n > 2\), \((k\text{-CNF}(n) \cup k\text{-DNF}(n))\) is a proper subset of \(k\text{-DL}(n)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Our proof makes essential use of the notion of the prime implicants of a Boolean function \(f\). An implicant of \(f\) is any term that logically implies \(f\). A prime implicant is an implicant all of whose literals are essential: if any literal is dropped it is no longer an implicant. For example, for the function represented by Table 1, \(\overline{x}_1\overline{x}_3\overline{x}_4\) is a prime implicant. We will also use the fact that if a function \(f\) has a prime implicant of size \(t\), then \(f\) has no \(k\)-DNF representation if \(k < t\). It follows from this that if the negation of a function \(f\) has a prime implicant of size \(t\), then \(f\) has no \(k\)-CNF representation if \(k < t\).

We break our proof into the cases \(k=1\) and \(1 < k < n\). For \(k=1\), we claim that the following decision list from \(1\text{-DL}(n)\) represents a function \(f\) that is not in \(1\text{-CNF}(n) \cup 1\text{-DNF}(n)\):

\[(x_1,0),(x_2,1),(x_3,1),(\mathrm{true},0). \tag{3}\]

To see that \(f\) is not in \(1\text{-DNF}(n)\), note that \(\overline{x}_1x_2\) is a prime implicant of \(f\). Similarly, to see that \(f\) is not in \(1\text{-CNF}(n)\), note that \(\overline{x}_2\overline{x}_3\) is a prime implicant of the negation of \(f\).

For \(1 < k < n\), we consider the decision list:

\[(\overline{x}_1x_{k+1},1),(x_1\overline{x}_{k+1},0),(x_1\mathbin{\oplus}x_2\mathbin{\oplus}\cdots\mathbin{\oplus}x_k,1),(\mathrm{true},0). \tag{4}\]

This is not a proper decision list according to our definition, since the third item does not have a term as its first part, but rather the “exclusive-or” of \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\), where “\(\oplus\)” denotes the exclusive-or (mod 2 addition) operator. However, it is simple to convert the function into a proper \(k\)-DL representation, since we are only taking the exclusive-or of \(k\) variables. The conversion would involve replacing the third item above with a sequence of \(2^{k-1}\) items whose first parts are all possible terms of length \(k\) involving variables \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\) having an odd number of unnegated variables, and whose second parts are always 1.

The function \(f\) represented above has the prime implicant

\[x_1\overline{x}_2\overline{x}_3\cdots\overline{x}_k x_{k+1}, \tag{5}\]

so that it has no \(k\)-DNF representation. Similarly, its negation \(\overline{f}\) has the prime implicant

\[\overline{x}_1\overline{x}_2\overline{x}_3\cdots\overline{x}_k\overline{x}_{k+1}, \tag{6}\]

so that \(f\) has no \(k\)-CNF representation either. ■

theorems.title: Decision lists generalize decision trees
theorems.statementHtml: \(k\text{-DT}(n)\) is a proper subset of \(k\text{-DL}(n)\), for \(0 < k < n\).
theorems.proofHtml:
By theorem 1 it suffices to observe that

\[k\text{-DT}(n) \subset k\text{-DNF}(n), \tag{7}\]

since one can create a \(k\text{-DNF}(n)\) formula equivalent to a given decision tree in \(k\text{-DT}(n)\) by creating one term for each leaf labelled with “1”. One could also create a \(k\text{-CNF}(n)\) formula equivalent to the given tree by creating one clause for each leaf labelled with “0”; this additional fact suffices to prove that \(k\text{-DT}(n) \subset k\text{-CNF}(n) \cap k\text{-DNF}(n)\). ■

theorems.title: Probability of a bad consistent hypothesis
theorems.statementHtml:
Given a function \(f \in \mathcal{F}_n\) and a sample \(S\) of \(f\) of size \(m\) drawn according to \(P_n\), the probability is at most

\[|\mathcal{F}_n|(1-\epsilon)^m\]

that there exists a hypothesis \(g \in \mathcal{F}_n\) such that

  • the error of \(g\) is greater than \(\epsilon\), and
  • \(g\) is consistent with \(S\).
theorems.proofHtml: If \(g\) is a single hypothesis in \(\mathcal{F}_n\) of error greater than \(\epsilon\), the chance that \(g\) is consistent with a randomly drawn sample \(S\) of size \(m\) is less than \((1-\epsilon)^m\). Since \(\mathcal{F}_n\) has \(|\mathcal{F}_n|\) members, the chance that there exists any member of \(\mathcal{F}_n\) satisfying both conditions above is at most \(|\mathcal{F}_n|(1-\epsilon)^m\). ■
